name: containers
context: Run, manage, and interact with Docker containers including lifecycle, resource limits, and health monitoring
info: The containers skill covers the full lifecycle of Docker containers: creating and running containers from images, managing container state (start, stop, restart, pause, remove), executing commands inside running containers, viewing and following logs, setting resource constraints (CPU, memory, storage), configuring restart policies, and monitoring container health. A container is a running instance of an image with its own filesystem, networking, and process namespace. Containers are ephemeral by default -- use volumes for persistent data and networks for communication. Sub-skills break down into run (creating and starting containers), exec (running commands in running containers), and logs (viewing container output). This skill focuses on direct container management; see [[docker/compose]] for multi-container orchestration.
body:
# Docker Containers

A Docker container is a running instance of an image. It has its own isolated filesystem, network interfaces, and process tree. Containers are created from images with `docker run` or `docker create`, and their lifecycle is managed with start, stop, restart, pause, unpause, and remove commands.

## Container Lifecycle

```
Image  --docker create-->  Created  --docker start-->  Running
                                                          |
       docker run = create + start                   docker stop
                                                          |
                                    docker rm  <--   Stopped/Exited
```

### State Transitions

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| `created` | Container exists but has not started |
| `running` | Main process is active |
| `paused` | Process frozen with SIGSTOP |
| `restarting` | Being restarted by restart policy or manual restart |
| `exited` | Main process has terminated |
| `dead` | Failed to stop cleanly |

## Managing Containers

```bash
# List running containers
docker ps

# List all containers (including stopped)
docker ps -a

# List with custom format
docker ps --format 'table {{.ID}}\t{{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}'

# Stop a running container (sends SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after 10s)
docker stop myapp
docker stop --time 30 myapp  # wait 30s before SIGKILL

# Start a stopped container
docker start myapp

# Restart a container
docker restart myapp

# Pause/unpause (freeze/unfreeze processes)
docker pause myapp
docker unpause myapp

# Remove a stopped container
docker rm myapp

# Force remove a running container
docker rm -f myapp

# Remove all stopped containers
docker container prune
```

## Resource Constraints

Limit CPU, memory, and I/O to prevent containers from consuming all host resources:

```bash
# Memory limit (hard) and reservation (soft)
docker run -d --memory 512m --memory-reservation 256m myapp

# CPU limit (number of cores)
docker run -d --cpus 1.5 myapp

# CPU shares (relative weight, default 1024)
docker run -d --cpu-shares 512 myapp

# Combined limits
docker run -d \
  --memory 1g \
  --memory-swap 2g \
  --cpus 2 \
  --pids-limit 100 \
  myapp

# View resource usage
docker stats
docker stats myapp --no-stream
```

## Restart Policies

Restart policies control what happens when a container exits:

| Policy | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `no` | Never restart (default) |
| `on-failure[:max]` | Restart only on non-zero exit code, with optional retry limit |
| `always` | Always restart regardless of exit code |
| `unless-stopped` | Like always, but not if manually stopped |

```bash
# Restart on failure up to 5 times
docker run -d --restart on-failure:5 myapp

# Always restart (survives Docker daemon restart)
docker run -d --restart always myapp

# Update restart policy on existing container
docker update --restart unless-stopped myapp
```

## Container Inspection

```bash
# Full JSON metadata
docker inspect myapp

# Specific fields
docker inspect --format '{{.State.Status}}' myapp
docker inspect --format '{{.NetworkSettings.IPAddress}}' myapp
docker inspect --format '{{json .Config.Env}}' myapp

# Process list inside container
docker top myapp

# Filesystem changes since image
docker diff myapp

# Port mappings
docker port myapp
```

## Container Health

If the image defines a `HEALTHCHECK`, Docker monitors container health:

```bash
# Check health status
docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' myapp
# Returns: starting, healthy, or unhealthy

# View health check log
docker inspect --format '{{json .State.Health}}' myapp | jq
```

In [[docker/compose]], use `depends_on` with `condition: service_healthy` to wait for health before starting dependent services.

## Cleanup

```bash
# Remove all stopped containers
docker container prune

# Remove containers older than 24 hours
docker container prune --filter 'until=24h'

# Full system cleanup
docker system prune -a --volumes
```

sections.title: Container lifecycle
sections.body: Containers transition through states: created, running, paused, restarting, exited, dead. docker run combines create and start. docker stop sends SIGTERM then SIGKILL after a timeout. docker rm removes stopped containers. docker container prune removes all stopped containers. Restart policies automate recovery from crashes.

sections.title: Resource constraints
sections.body: Limit container resources with --memory (hard limit), --memory-reservation (soft limit), --cpus (CPU cores), --cpu-shares (relative weight), --pids-limit (process count), and --memory-swap (memory + swap). Monitor with docker stats. Update limits on running containers with docker update. Prevents noisy neighbors on shared hosts.

sections.title: Restart policies
sections.body: Four restart policies: no (default), on-failure (with optional retry limit), always (survives daemon restart), unless-stopped (like always but respects manual stop). Set with --restart on docker run. Update existing containers with docker update --restart. In Compose, use the restart key on services.

sections.title: Container inspection
sections.body: docker inspect returns full JSON metadata. Use --format with Go templates to extract specific fields: state, IP address, environment variables, mounts, port mappings. docker top shows processes. docker diff shows filesystem changes. docker port shows port mappings. docker stats shows live resource usage.

sections.title: Health monitoring
sections.body: Containers with a HEALTHCHECK instruction report health status: starting, healthy, or unhealthy. Docker runs the health check command at configured intervals. Inspect health with docker inspect --format on .State.Health. In Compose, depends_on with condition: service_healthy ensures dependencies are ready.

scripts.name: List Containers
scripts.description: List Docker containers with status, ports, and resource usage
scripts.inline: echo '=== Running Containers ===' && docker ps --format 'table {{.ID}}\t{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}' && if [ "$SHOW_ALL" = 'true' ]; then echo '' && echo '=== Stopped Containers ===' && docker ps -a --filter 'status=exited' --format 'table {{.ID}}\t{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}'; fi && if [ "$SHOW_STATS" = 'true' ]; then echo '' && echo '=== Resource Usage ===' && docker stats --no-stream --format 'table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}\t{{.NetIO}}\t{{.BlockIO}}'; fi

SHOW_ALL.description: Set to 'true' to include stopped containers

SHOW_STATS.description: Set to 'true' to show resource usage statistics

scripts.name: Stop Container
scripts.description: Gracefully stop a running Docker container with configurable timeout
scripts.inline: echo "Stopping container '${CONTAINER}' (timeout: ${TIMEOUT:-10}s)..." && docker stop --time "${TIMEOUT:-10}" "$CONTAINER" && echo "Container '${CONTAINER}' stopped" && docker inspect --format 'Exit code: {{.State.ExitCode}}' "$CONTAINER"

CONTAINER.description: Container name or ID to stop

scripts.name: Container Stats
scripts.description: Display live or snapshot resource usage for containers
scripts.inline: if [ -n "$CONTAINER" ]; then docker stats ${NO_STREAM:+--no-stream} --format 'table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}\t{{.MemPerc}}\t{{.NetIO}}\t{{.BlockIO}}\t{{.PIDs}}' "$CONTAINER"; else docker stats ${NO_STREAM:+--no-stream} --format 'table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}\t{{.MemPerc}}\t{{.NetIO}}\t{{.BlockIO}}\t{{.PIDs}}'; fi

CONTAINER.description: Specific container name or ID (default: all running)

NO_STREAM.description: Set to any value for a single snapshot instead of live stream

scripts.name: Cleanup Containers
scripts.description: Remove stopped containers and optionally prune system resources
scripts.inline: echo '=== Stopped Containers ===' && docker ps -a --filter 'status=exited' --format 'table {{.ID}}\t{{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}' && echo '' && echo 'Removing stopped containers...' && docker container prune -f ${OLDER_THAN:+--filter "until=$OLDER_THAN"} && if [ "$FULL_PRUNE" = 'true' ]; then echo '' && echo 'Running full system prune...' && docker system prune -f ${PRUNE_VOLUMES:+--volumes}; fi

OLDER_THAN.description: Only remove containers older than this duration (e.g. 24h, 7d)

FULL_PRUNE.description: Set to 'true' to also prune images, networks, and build cache

PRUNE_VOLUMES.description: Set to 'true' to also prune volumes (data loss warning)

timeout.description: Stop timeout in seconds before SIGKILL

removed.description: Number of containers removed in prune

depends.reason: Docker Engine must be installed and running

author: dojo-community
